lines.st: Dad, why's there a knife on the wall?
That was my constant companion when I was young and begging for food.
So why's there an empty shelf under it?
That's reserved for an old elder of mine.
What happened to them?
We had a falling out.
Why?
Because...

lines.x: It's early in the morning, and the weather's cloudy.
Along Yingfeng Road in Shangshu of Great Yan, sits an inn.
Not many establishments would call themselves an inn in modern Yan.
But this one has the most antique atmosphere, the finest wine and tea, and is the most cultured in all Shangshu.
Its sign is red with gold lettering.
'Xingyu.'
Ten jars of Husong, three jars of Yunliao, six jars of Guixing reserve... hmm...
Liu, why are our numbers here so much lower than across the river?
Don't blame me! They went crazy with banquets at the start of the month. Of course they sold more than us!
How much difference can a few parties make?
Aiyo wei! You don't even know the half of it.
You were out of Shangshu then. You don't know how ridiculous that hoo-hah was... Those two days of business could pay half a year's worth of bills, no exaggeration.
We were beating them for months, and those two days completely sunk us. You can't keep letting them make a fool of us, Mr. Zheng.
Remember, we were here first, and were the first to do it well. If you ask me the other places should be bowing to my old man here.
Don't be fooled into loving your grandkids while you forget your son here.
What are you even blubbering about? Since when am I your old man? I haven't even said anything.
Yeah, and it'll be all over for me once you do.
It was just bad luck. If I were the one to meet those nobles...
If it's just bad luck, then where's all your good luck to balance it out?
Gah! Nobles, nobles, where did all those nobles come from...?
We've always relied on word of mouth from our regulars, and you won't even do ads. If we could just invite a few pretty boys and girls to...
(Glare) ...
You heard nothing. That was the wind just now.
Running a restaurant-inn depends on customers with taste and connections, true. But what do you know about who's connected and who isn't?
If I knew, I'd be running the inn.
Then sit tight and learn.
Anyone with eyes can discern if someone rich, powerful, stylish, and wise, that's plain as day. So why can't you?
Erk...
Whether you're a kung fu adventurer or a settled person, so long as you're alive you'll mingle with plenty of other people.
So keep mingling, and you'll get a good eye for this. Knowing people, and knowing what they know, is what lets us live well.
Meanwhile, you spend all day fiddling about, and run off to the mahjong parlors every night. What kind of future do you expect when...
Why'd you stop the lecture?
Don't you see our two guests at the door?! Why aren't you greeting them?!
Aiyo... I completely missed them! Quick, quick, get them in! The first two customers of the day! Get them two plates of salted peanuts and a pot of chrysanthemum tea!
Might our luck be looking up?
We are early. Let me treat you to dinner in Mr. Liang's stead.
Take a seat! Tea will be with you in a moment!
I thought we'd have made a beeline for his residence.
Your 'friend' there is still a magistrate. Busy, in any case.
Busy enough to overlook a friend? Must be busy as can be.
(Sir, sir, what do you think, then? Are these two noble-types?)
Pah.
Don't ask, observe. You can't judge at first glance.

lines.st: Shangshu, where the winds blow high, and the clouds part to show off the moon.
The land was mountainous since times untold. Some would say the rugged terrain posed quite the obstacle to the nomadic city's development.
But the folk of Shangshu were loath to give up the land they'd dwelt in since antiquity, so they went to great pains to move mountains and fuse their city with the peaks.
Hence, unlike other cities, Shangshu's plates were distributed along the lines of the range, each perhaps hundreds or thousands of meters wide and utterly unique in their scenery.

How auspicious Shangshu's habitation must be that in recent years it has never once been struck by Catastrophe. Its people have built new towns in the mountains, and opened them up with ropeways and tunnels.
Furthermore, with the guidance of the Tumu Tianshi, a mountain range of particular renown was directly relocated onto the nomadic platforms, where...

lines.x: Slow down mate, what's a Tumu... Tianshi? 'Heavenly Master of Earth and Wood'?

You might be unaware, benefactor, but Yan has multiple bureaus of tianshi, Celestial Masters. Any student of the land even a mite accomplished at Originium Arts may apply to join, take layer upon layer of examination, and so serve the country.

Originium Arts have a dizzying array of applications, and the science of Originium is developing by the day. The tianshi of Yan are naturally classified by profession and specialty—civil engineers, for example! The artists of earth and wood indeed.
And here I thought cool titles like that would be reserved for Casters like Leizi—y'know, ones with crazy battle prowess.
Would Leizi be that Yan official in Rhodes Island you mentioned before? My my, it may have been like that in the past, but times have changed. Now the welfare of the people comes first.
Originium technology benefits the masses, and every tianshi of Yan's Ministry of Works pours their life's blood into researching Originium's Arts and testing new materials.
Other countries couldn't dream of moving whole mountains onto nomadic cities, that's for sure...
Indeed. While landscapes may soothe the soul, it certainly wouldn't be worth such monumental efforts if it broke the backs and the banks of the people in the process.

Still, most of the credit belongs to the civil engineers of the tianshi who loved their home enough to put in the work. And moving mountains was easy compared to convincing the local villages and estates to agree to the project.
Yan has an old proverb. It's a land that raises its people.
A shame I can't speak much on Originium Arts, or else I'd have the same things on my mind as one of those nomadic city architects.
Is everyone from Yan like this?
It's these rivers and hills before us, see—
The limitless sights. Early springs so tranquil you can hear the snowflakes land. There's a lasting charm about it.
If you'd lived here since the cradle too, perhaps you'd think the same way.
Maybe. Who can say?
Phew—now that's refreshing.
'Where the winds blow high, and the clouds part to show off the moon.' I can see how it earned that.
Within Shangshu lie the renowned 'Three Mounts and Seventeen Peaks,' and they draw plenty of tourists. Some foreign nobility even make the trip out here simply to admire them.
Presumably the common folk owe it only to the pains of crossing the land that these landmarks aren't swarmed by such visitors coming in droves.
There's a comfy breeze up here. I was worried it'd rain and delay us from heading up the mountains today.
Quite right, benefactor. The spring breeze caresses one's cheeks, the drizzle falls finely, all silent and slick in the rain.
Been meaning to ask, actually. You're a martial artist, right? So how'd you get so... bookish?

My master often instructed us that practicing the martial arts hardly precludes the study of culture. Indeed, the arts come both martial and cultural. To learn them well is but the duty of the modern soul.
Besides, the art of gab can be a gateway to making some money.
Benefactor, what say we visit the nearest summit on the way? I hear more people have taken to the mountains these last two years; there's been quite the hubbub.
Pass. We'll rest in the city for a day or two, wait for the sisters to meet up with us, and head on our way...
*Sigh* Never felt so bad about missing all the scenery on the way though.
You've always been singularly devoted, benefactor. A mind only for the objective, not a whit more. And as proof of your conscientiousness, you never take a break.
Never take breaks? Me?
But of course!
Whatever, flattery will get you nowhere. We're not going.
Er, but you're merely taking me to the office for onboarding. Such a minor errand needn't be so urgent.
And did I not hear Lava mention you undertook this journey intending a vacation? Surely nothing impedes you from a good tour of the scenery then?
*Sigh*.
If only Lava were here... I'm not the only one who needs to sort out her head right now.
Benefactor Lava just happened to be tasked with a sudden commission taking her a different way—I imagine she'll pass by scenery just as fine.
I don't think she's the type to gawp at the view. Not in a million years.
Haha, so she isn't.
To be honest though, she wasn't always this way. Me neither, of course.
You needn't brood, benefactor. Things change, we change. The world's affairs are inconstant.
Let's hurry on our way, then.
Found them, Miss Du.
You sure?
No mistake. They came in at Zhengshan Crossing, and got to Yingfeng Road in Weichang District. He got someone reliable to come with him the whole way.
And the Lungmenite's carrying an antique little box on him, correct?
Yep. Clear as day.
If anything goes wrong, you're responsible.
You left this to us. We won't let it go wrong.
Good!
Then we're paying this buccaneer a visit in place of those yellow-bellied oldies!

The inn has over ten branch locations in Shangshu. Yet, they do not take the name Xingyu. Perhaps Xinglu for a journey fortunate, or Xingfu for a journey blessed—but Xingyu, for a journey plentiful, belongs to this inn alone.

And of course, this inn alone provides such an incredible view of the skyscrapers against the twin moons mirrored in the distant lake at night. The innkeeper paid mind only to the scenery when choosing locations. Not to the guests, nor to the rent.
Fine scenery puts guests in a fine mood. Fine scenery puts all in harmony, and the coin will come whether you like it or not.
Mmm... fine tea indeed, and a fine view.
First timers should stay somewhere nice. Get a good impression of Shangshu.
Xingyu Inn. An antique, elegant name.
Famous, but it only recently got into food service. The innkeeper has talent, and whatever he touches goes well.
It's grown to be a lot to handle, but still he...
Is that him there, busying about?
The very same.
Doesn't look like an owner at all. Very steady as he goes. You have to admire him.
If only I knew what my old schoolmate was up to... The Liang boy.
To borrow your words: steady as he goes.
Is he a good mandarin?
Ask any passerby, shoe-shiner, yam hawker, or taxi driver on the street. They'd all give him a thumbs-up.
Haha. Living his dream, then. Wish I could say that.
You haven't met Mr. Liang in over ten years?
Nope.
But he still chose you to deliver this item.
That it is.
Then your bond must deserve a thumbs-up as well.
I'm not experienced enough to say what this portends. I only know by Mr. Liang's expression that this is no laughing matter.
Hardly. I wouldn't dare flaunt any grand bond with your 'Mr. Liang.' But Liang Xun, the man? He's a friend.
A friend, you say?
And a good one.
A good friend is hard enough to find, much less one that will stay so after ten years apart.
What about you, Master Shen?
What about me?
With so many ferrymen at the docks, didn't it seem strange I chose you in particular?
No. I was briefed by Mr. Liang, so I assumed you were too. I have no need for the details.
I had to steal this thing right out of a smuggler's hands for old Mr. Liang. Took some effort.
I didn't ask. You must have been itching to speak.
I came all this way with no one to talk to. You bet I'm itchy.
I'm surprised you trust Liang, what with that cold look on his face. Most people don't.
Ah. You trust him because he's your friend. I do because he's the magistrate of Shangshu.
Do you know him well?
I do. Mr. Liang is good to us, so we return the favor.
How long have you been in this business, Master Shen?
Hard to say... Who counts out the passing years on their fingers? Twenty? Thirty?
Very admirable.
I'm only a boatman, what's there to admire? Lately they've been putting engines on the boats. Maybe I'll be out of a job in a few years.
They'll still need someone to navigate the route and keep watch on the sky. I figure as long as there are waterways and the years run long, boatmen and Catastrophe Messengers are in the same boat.
Catastrophes, you say.
You don't see one often, but when you do, you never forget.
Have you seen one before?
I have, but also things even more frightening. Not on the rivers, mind you—wait, what have you got me saying?
Hm.
What is it?
Strange. Two foreigners, it seems.
Alright, two guests?
Two guests.
So this is your 'reputation exceptional, dishes quintessential, service congenial, treatment all-equal' Xingyu Inn, then?
But of course, but of course. My master had nothing but praise for the 'Xingyu' name, so while I've never been graced with a chance to visit Shangshu, I just had to take you here, benefactor.
Ah, but...
But?
Heh, it shames me to admit, but my master praised only the name, not any particular amenity. So I am also ignorant as to its precise forte.
Here's your tea.
Just refreshments, or will you two be needing lodging too?
Wow, they've even got the standard line, huh?
Hey, listen here. Maybe we've only been open for a few years, but trace the name 'Xingyu' back and you'll see it's got a century's worth of fame.
Benefactor, we've been searching for lodging, the prices are suitable enough, and it's not far from the northern crossing we agreed to meet at. What say you?
I'm fine with whatever.
Then two rooms, please.
Allllright then. Anything to eat for now?
May I ask what you have?
Our recommended set meals are on the signs. We're currently on the afternoon menu. If you need anything, just shout.
Very well, let's see...
This one looks alright, mate. Lot of Yan flavor in that name.
The extra-spicy fowl blood curd set...? I'd recommend refraining for now.
Why? When in Londinium, right? Can't hurt to try.
In my experience, such dishes don't quite... mesh with the lunchtime sensibilities a Rim Billitonian such as yourself might be accustomed to.
That so? I think I'm pretty good with spice, myself.
What are you sneaking peeks at?
Look there, at the proprietor. The innkeeper, I wager.
What about him?
He's a force to be reckoned with.
A force how?
Of course, he has to be, with a business this grand. But there's something more than that...
Are those calluses on his hands?
Your vision is as astounding as ever, benefactor!
And there's been two guests on the floor upstairs eyeing us ever since we walked in.
Hm?
What part of them's caught your eye?
Well... that Cautus seems oddly familiar...
Are you staying here today, boss? Not heading to check out the other side of the crossing?
No, I'm expecting someone. I must be here to attend to this guest.
Heh, you're offering personal service? Gotta be a big shot then. Are we open for business then? How much can we make?
There might not be money in it. No shortage of things to settle, though.
Is it about the business over there?
Yes and no.
What does THAT mean?
Less blabbering, more greeting the customers.
Pssh, penny-pincher.
Ai, welcome, guests, are you—
...Hmph.
Miss Du? What are you—
None of your business. Out of the way.
Hey!
Why—what a formidable greeting, young lady. Are you looking for me?
A sloppy man with glasses, a gaudy outfit, and the look of a swindler.
So it's gotta be you, right?
Er... I must say that appraisal is a little...
An enemy of yours?
No, they'd never go so far as to send a little girl to do their bidding...
Who's a little girl, huh?!
(She kicked up an entire wooden chair—what leg technique!)
Hmph. You have quick reflexes, dodging without getting up.
Miss, Miss!
What? Can't you see I'm busy—
This guy's a Liberi, right? Didn't the letter say we were looking for a Lung?
Then it's the one upstairs. It's you, right, isn't it?!
Hey now, don't I deserve at least an apology?
Cool it, mate... Hm?
Personally, I thought my attire was just dandy.
Given her approach, I doubt she means well.
You stroll in and abuse an innocent chair, not to mention almost giving that man a concussion for no reason.
Give it up, your cover's blown. Now play nice and hand it over.
Hmph. I'll grant that it takes guts to sneak into the city in broad daylight though.
Miss Du, was it? I believe there might be a misunderstanding.
Did you come from Lungmen?
Er, yes.
Are you carrying an antique wine cup with you?
Then you have one minute to think up a good excuse. After that, you're handing it over and coming with me.
The young lady's not too keen on hearing people out... Really makes me miss those kids of mine back home. They'd know better.
Don't look at me. Mr. Liang explained that he specifically entrusted you with bringing that wine cup from Lungmen.
And if Mr. Liang says so, I believe it.
Well, Master Shen, thanks for the vote of confidence.
Odd though... This girl's bold enough to openly say she wants something that Mr. Liang requested?
*Sigh*.
One of our aunties plucked this right from a child smuggler's hands, so if you were expecting any legal formalities... haha.
Is the cup noteworthy?
I looked into it, there's only baseless legends. Rumors the smugglers heard from the locals.
*Sigh*. How did this become such a hassle?
(Nothing, mate, I've seen this in a movie before!)
(As have I! But they'd usually call the officers at this point. They'd never jump into a fight, right...?)
Hey, enough muttering! Got your excuse ready?
(The lady is certainly blessed with a loud voice... Might those two upstairs be suspects of a sort, then?)
(Benefactor?)
(Mate. I... I think that guy looks familiar.)
(What? The fellow with the remarkably similar get-up to mine?)
(No... It can't be... They work with Rhodes Island. Guess there's a little resemblance though?)
How about this, young lady? You've caught me, fair and square. Nowhere to run. Now, if you'd call the police...
Stalling for time, huh? Alright. I was wondering how long you could anyway.
Get up there!
Don't let him get away!
A moment, please!
What, you want to pay me back for earlier?
Stand down, let me finish my business, and I'll pay for two days of lodging, all expenses. Sound good?
Take a good, long look Benefactor. If we've not got the wrong person, then a wonderful opportunity has landed in our laps.
Mr. Lee...?
Ah...
Let's see... little blonde rabbit, ambiguously closed eyes... a Rhodes Island operator?
Miraculous. Of the taverns in Shangshu, in all of Yan, in all the world, to think you'd have the fortune to run into an old friend here.
So how about we not sour the mood on this miraculous reunion?
What the hell is going on?! You're all together?!
Surround them! Nobody leaves until they hand over the booty!
Oh? What inspired this?
I dreamt of the fable of the shadow's shadow counseling its shade, and this is my response.
Then I'd like to see it. How do you plan to let these common objects manifest themselves?
Take it as but an inadvertent act. Mock me not for it.
What the... just now...
Benefactor, watch out!
They're all civvies. Keep a light touch.
Careful, that Liberi's from some martial school! Leave the Cautus to me—huh? Where'd she go?
Why's it always punch first, ask questions later...?
Wh-When did she get on the second floor?!
No need to hurt anyone, Nothing, mate. Easy does it.
Over here, Mr. Lee.
*Sigh* So sorry to get you all caught up in this mess.
Not like we expected to run into you, Mr. Lee. Well, uh, let's get out of here first.
Hey—! Are you ignoring me on purpose?! You think you can just leave?!
Apologies, but you won't be passing.
And who're you to decide?!
A fan? That swindler... uh, no, that guest knows some rare kung fu, doesn't he?
Zheng? Sir? Shouldn't you step in before they ruin all the furniture on the ground floor?
*Sigh*...
Fan man's moves are probably meant to intimidate. He's being very slick about it. But the lady hasn't shown us everything yet either. If they work things out, we all come out richer and happier.
While she's still being cautious, at least.
Hey!
All you fine people!
In the late evenings of Shangshu's early spring, the wind arrives on time while the rain is no guarantee. So it has always been.
A man lifts his head to the skies. The clouds and mist furl, Hui and Ming waver, but at the furthest ends, one can faintly see the light of day.
A spring chill hangs, and a light breeze flutters their clothes.
He should be here by now.
I do hope that things conclude smoothly.
...Cheh.
Hm? What's this pause for?
Don't tell me I was a little too heavy-handed, and hurt you in my carelessness, young lady?
Hmph. So you know some flowery kung fu. Happy?
Your buccaneer's already fled on us, so there's no sense tangling with you any longer, you—
—What's your name?
Nothing. The latter man of Tzu-hsu and Wu-yu.
Keh. What kind of conman gives his real name? That must be some stupid kung fu stage name.
Ah, how keen-witted you are.
So you're not going to let me past to chase them, right?
Hence why I compliment your keen wit.
Shut it with your flapping!
What are you waiting for?! Let's take him!
Miss! We'll help you out!
—Hold it!
Eep...
Ahem.
This is still an inn. I can't have you two making a mess of the restaurant and fools of us all. What will the passersby think?
...Cheh.
However, you two fought marvelously. In honor of your eye-opening display, I... won't ask you to cover the costs.
But for now, please rein it in. Call it a day. For my sake.
Hmph.
Do pardon us, good proprietor. We'll resolve this misunderstanding forthwith, then make amends at your door.
I'll let you go... this time. For the innkeep.
I don't know how you're involved with that guy, but I'd hand him over if I were you.
Cover for him and you're an accomplice. You're free to go for now, but Shangshu doesn't tolerate outlaws.
If this were simply a matter of outlawry, you could have called the police. What has you resorting to force, Miss Du?
Nothing is right. No matter the reason, a mere disagreement shouldn't bring you to fisticuffs.
Why you...!
Now, now, Miss.
Let's have your people leave.
M-Miss, do we just let him go...? We could grab him, then wait for the Lungmenite to take the bait—
Quiet.
We've already let the Lungmenite escape. Keeping this guy with us all night wouldn't be much use.
Besides, could you even restrain him? And you, you're still holding back some moves, aren't you?
Hardly. Friendship is wealth, now, friendship is wealth. If the young lady here is willing to take a step back, then I couldn't ask for anything more...
I won't forget this, Nothing.
Please, don't waste your memories on me. The esteemed have so much to forget.
(Carefully, carefully... Good, I'll get a decent head start from here.)
I'm only listening to Mr. Zheng—if you want to see how things play out outside, be my guest. I suggest you sit tight and listen to—
—Hey! Where'd he go?!
H-How did this happen?! What are you all DOING?!
Er... you weren't exactly clear on whether we were letting him go, Miss...
Don't just stand there! After him!
Y-Yes, Miss!
Hey, Miss Du, don't you think you're making too big of a mess?
Out of my way!
Ai, ai, don't—whatever, this isn't my problem anymore...
And Dad! Why were acting like this doesn't concern you? You even stopped me!
You were making trouble.
Trouble, my ass! You knew they'd go through Yingfeng Road, right? Isn't that why you were here waiting for them?!
We've got eyes in dozens of inns and eateries in this city! Catching some blind idiot of a thief's not hard—
Who's the blind idiot here?
You don't know what that wine cup is, let alone what that Lungmenite might have up his sleeve.
Not to mention how capable those people were. If they were willing to cause a commotion, they'd have won, and come out looking reasonable too.
But––
But what? Do you know just how significant their mission is? Or who the anonymous client is?
You think you're all it takes to put hired escorts to shame?
Looks like no one's following.
Sorry everyone. I should've been leading the way, but here we are in this plight.
I never imagined something like this would await us in Shangshu City though...
Was it really alright to leave that young man alone? Have we abandoned him?
The innkeeper and the pushy woman seemed to know each other. I'm worried our rearguard hero might not slip away so easily.
Huh... how do you figure, Mr. Lee?
She was clearly mindful of him while making her move. That'd never fool me.
Get fooled in my line of work, and you're out business.
Oh...
Then we can only hope he's a fast runner. And honestly, I'd vouch for that.
Will he really be okay?
He'll be right as rain. The girl wouldn't dare to make a scene in broad daylight, right? It's a quiet street, but SOMEONE's gonna notice.
Phew... That's a relief. But fancy meeting you Rhodes Islanders here.
And what brings you to Shangshu, Mr. Lee? Commission at your office?
I suppose. Just a private matter entrusted to me by an old friend though.
Is it that box?
That it is.
But this truly is bizarre. Why would anyone in Shangshu dare to steal Mr. Liang's property?
It's not bizarre at all. If we could slip into Shangshu without a fuss, why would our Mr. Liang have bothered to seek me out?
Mr. Liang?
An old schoolmate... You can think of him as the Wei Yenwu of Shangshu.
Wow then... Mr. Wei, huh...
By the sound of it, you all know Lord Wei of Lungmen quite well?
Maybe the average man on the street wouldn't, but those of us sifting through Lungmen's scraps? Sure, we all do.
Recognizing Lord Wei and being recognized by him are very different things.
You're not wrong, but let's leave me and Wei Yenwu's somewhat rocky relationship out of this.
Ah. A shame I'll never meet anyone of Lord Wei's caliber.
Sure, he's extraordinary, but not THAT extraordinary.
You're kinda making it sound like Mr. Liang's no big deal by comparison.
Does he count as a big deal?
He does. Why wouldn't he?
Wait, do you mean Lord Wei of Lungmen doesn't, or Mr. Liang doesn't?
Then I suppose everyone's a big deal these days, tut-tut-tut. Back then, he was just a hothead who didn't even know the meaning of the word 'adapt'.
You're the same as ever, huh Mr. Lee?
Hah. What's it matter now, with me eking out my days in Lungmen while he officiates all of Shangshu? We haven't met in forever.
Look, there. It's that young man.
Nothing! Alright, mate?
Ow, ow, ow, ow! Hands off, I'm aching all over...

You may not realize this, but that young girl just now went utterly berserk! Merely warding her off tested every bone in me, and it was only by the proprietor's timely intervention that I had a gap through which to scurry—
With the way you came running over, you're obviously completely fine.
Fan tucked in hand and everything. So care to repeat that? Tested every bone?
Erk... There's no fooling you, benefactor... I will say, though, it's been far too long since I last used so many moves. The muscle ache alone is...
And this young man is?
Operator Mr. Nothing.
I may not have formally taken the post, but my devoted heart and tireless efforts have long since beaten to Rhodes Island's tune.
So you're a new operator friend. Good to meet you.

Well met, well met. I hear from my benefactor that you're also an ally to Rhodes Island? Ah, as expected of Rhodes Island, friend to all and beloved by the masses, that they'd strike an accord with a fine soul like yourself, er...
Just call me old Lee.
How kind of you, Brother Lee.
Well then, brother. You say you only got out once the innkeeper intervened?
Precisely so. What of it?
Hmm... seems things aren't as simple as I thought.
This isn't the place to stand and chat, too many ears. I'd never forgive myself for another setback.
Then we'll head straight for the Liang estate.
As for our friends...
I was only told to meet with you. Bringing any more could be a little...
Ah, never mind.
I doubt Mr. Liang's so small-minded. And you did both help us out, so we'll go together.
The Liang estate, huh... I'd really rather not bother anyone else...
It's fine, you're with me.
Decisive words, Brother Lee.
Good... if that's settled, then let's be quick. Come, this way.
Brother Lee?
Hmm? Ah... I'll be right there.
Oh...
Ao?
What's this...? Are these strange pets the new trend in Shangshu?
Ao!
Brother Lee! Don't fall behind!
...I'm coming, I'm coming.
Ao...
The cold spell of springtide.
It hearkens me back to many scenes of the past: my academic pursuits, my career in business, my training in the martial arts, and my entrance into politics.
In those bygone times, we were inseparable. Kind friends and mentors were always there for us, each of them hoping to realize their own grand aspirations.
What about now?
The green bricks have yet to be cleaned. Spring has long since begun, yet the snow lingers, white as pearls.
It is impossible to know what the man behind the door thinks when it is shut. All you are certain of is that, when it opens, what you know is merely what he wants you to know.
He lifts his head. The door lets out a creak.
Mr. Liang.
Liang Xun... Long time no see.
Indeed. It's been a while.
You've ventured far, Lee.
It was a long journey, and I'd be lying if I said it wasn't arduous. How gaudy of you then, Mr. Liang, to have me travel all this way just to deliver this small trinket.
All will be explained as soon as I have the time.
I must thank you too, Master Shen. It's early spring. There must be many a traveler seeking to make their way across the waters.
Not at all. I accepted the job, so naturally it's my top priority.
But now that I've brought him... I think I'll head back to the docks.
Oh right. We ran into a bit of trouble in the city. Lee can fill you in on the details.
Trouble...?
You'd best be careful too, sir.
I'll take my leave. The docks must be low on manpower by now. I should hurry back.
Right. Thank you for your work.
'Make your way to Shangshu via Zhengshan, and find a boatman named Shen.'
See how far your reputation gets you, Mr. Liang? You sent for me with just that one sentence.
This isn't exactly your first time visiting Shangshu.
So what kind of man is this Master Shen?
Just a trustworthy boatman. And experienced boatmen can be trusted with your life.
Fair enough.
Come to think of it, I haven't been introduced to these two...
(Good gracious, how did I not notice? So this 'Mr. Liang' is the magistrate of Shangshu?!)
(I'm getting pretty used to you overreacting to stuff you missed. What's that mean for us, though?)
(He's the head of the city!)
(Knowing how Yan ranks officials, though, doesn't that make him a whole lot lower than Wei Yenwu?)
These two are my... business partners.
It is an honor, Mr. Liang. I'm Ch'u...
Wu-yu. Please call me Mr. Nothing.
And I'm Kroos. I'm an Operator on deployment from Rhodes Island Pharmaceuticals, which has an ongoing business relationship with Mr. Lee.
We only bumped into each other in Shangshu by chance, though. Anyway, it's a pleasure to meet you, Mr. Liang.
By chance? And here I thought you hired Messengers to escort you from Lungmen...
So you ARE aware that Messengers exist? Then perhaps you should've hired them to run your errands instead of me.
It was no trivial matter, and the journey was long. I couldn't trust just anyone.
But you could trust me? Give me a break. What was I supposed to do if I ran into bandits on the way?
If you thought it a hassle, you could have written back and refused.
Please, like there'd be another chance with that trinket of yours. By the time your letter made it to me, the smugglers were already in Lungmen.
When did I have time to write back and tell you to find somebody else? I had to get someone looking into them straight away, smashing up their hideout too, while we were at it.
*Sigh* Fortunately, the kids working for me are more than capable of holding the fort. Otherwise I wouldn't have time for this errand of yours.
(Sounds like Waai Fu's MO to me... and I'm guessing Mr. Lee took this as an excuse to skip town rather than deal with everything on his plate.)
Seeing as you are Lee's business partners, we shouldn't be discussing this at the door. Please, come in. We have enough guest rooms for everyone.
What an honor to be personally received by Shangshu's magistrate. Best we accept, then.
Please, this way.
You have our thanks, Mr. Liang.
Benefactor, we were just worrying about where to stay the night, and there's still some time until our meeting. Why not get some rest here?
Hmm...
Benefactor?
Nothing... Guess I'm just tired.
Mr. Lee, do we have to tell Mr. Liang about what just happened?
Yes, we should...
Look at the sky. It's filled with clouds. It'll probably rain tonight—
...? What's the matter, Lee?
Nothing...
Some landscapes flashed through my mind, is all... Maybe it's been way too long since I last visited Shangshu, and the nostalgia is getting to me.
Miss Du... How did it go?
How did what go? That ugly old Zheng gave me an earful.
What should we do, then?
I told you. We can't always listen to them.
If that's all we ever do, we won't have a future.
Just do as I say.
Alright. I'll call everyone up.
Mm-hm.
How will you deal with the Lungmenite now?
I'll go see him myself.
Some things are best discussed with an open heart.
How about a drink?
I'll pass. I have work later.
You had me travel thousands of li to bring you a wine cup, and you won't indulge me with one little drink?
I went through hell and high water for this thing, you know.
Drink makes fools of us all.
Ah, 'drink makes fools of us all...'
One of the Liang Xun of yesteryear's favorite sayings. Full glad I am to know that Mr. Liang still cannot hold his liquor.
And full glad I am to know that you're still that good old foulmouthed Lee.
I've sent someone to investigate what happened at Xingyu Inn.
I doubt things are as simple as they seem. If you really don't know, then no need to stir up the hornet's nest. Such is the society we live in.
Especially when simply waiting for me to leave the city would more or less solve all our troubles.
Are you sure you don't need to check on the cup? The roads were bumpy. I could very well have flattened it under my posterior.
If you didn't feel it worth mentioning, then it must be safe and sound.
Haha...
I say you take a look anyway. I hand-delivered this beauty all the way from Lungmen, after all.
So this is it...
Now, indulge my curiosity: what makes it so special? At the price it was going for in the smugglers' auctions, it may as well have been a buy-one-get-one-free sale.
A rumor circulating among the smugglers did catch my attention though. Apparently, this thing was at the center of a ghost story in the village it was found.
Do tell.
They say the cup can make the things around it... come to life.
Is there any proof?
If there were any proof to a ghost story, you'd have heard about it on the news by now. I only know the rumors.
And what of the smugglers?
All taken to the L.G.D.
That's good to hear.
Actually, there is another matter I would like you to take care of.
Well, you summoned me all the way from Lungmen. You may as well get your money's worth while I'm here.
But for now...
Hoh... It seems you won't let me go without sharing a drink with you.
We'll save business for later, then. You haven't changed, old friend. Carefree as always.
Come, sit.
Now you're the master?
How are your days in Lungmen? I heard you're a private investigator now?
Right... And Huai's daughter is staying with me, too. You know, Huai, the kung fu lunatic.
Ah, no wonder you asked me to look into his whereabouts all of a sudden.
I've been taking care of Waai Fu for a while now, actually. I even went to her university graduation ceremony in his place. Am I supposed to walk her down the aisle when she gets married too?
Waai Fu's a good girl, but she's got a terrible father.
His daughter... She's grown so much.
Time flies, Liang Xun.
Indeed...
I heard you took up a job in government, and with your personality and skills I figured you had a bright future ahead of you. Never did I imagine that our reunion would take so many years though.
I'm sure everyone will be happy to hear you managed to get back on your feet.
Hardly. I just realized that some things aren't nearly as much of a headache as figuring out how to put food on the table.
It's been a while since I had your cooking...
You're a big shot who governs a large city. Stop looking back so fondly on the tough times we struggled through.
Instead, think back to the times when we lacked opportunities to use our talents, and make sure the youngsters in your city have them. Isn't that what used to vex you the most?
Indeed...
Hmm, still, glad to see you're still the type to never, ever, hold a woman.
I was worried I'd come to Shangshu only to find you'd started a family. Which would make me—
Mr. Liang... You have a guest?
*Cough*...
Miss Ning... I'm afraid now is not a good time...
—What do you mean, now isn't a good time? I'm just a Messenger. I'll be taking my leave soon!
Good evening, Mr. Liang... and... Mr. Messenger?
That's right, young miss.
(Explain.)
(She's a coworker.)
(Mighty fine coworker you have over at this hour.)
(It's barely past dinnertime.)
Oh.
Might you have forgotten our engagement tonight?
No, it's just...
Please, don't mind me. I have a few errands that just came to mind.
I'll leave you to it, Mr. Liang. If you need anything else... Ugh, try to finish your business first.
Wait, Lee—
Well, seems I'm in trouble.
Nothing, mate... We're just looking for some grub. Does this really need to be such a big deal?
It's not like Nian's here. I'm fine with whatever. No one's going to complain.
Absolutely not, my benefactor! I must apologize for what happened earlier by finding you the very finest establishment!
Okay... so how come we're in some empty alley?
You don't understand. Everyone in Yan has a handful of hole-in-the-wall eateries they hold dear to their hearts. Flavors that simply cannot be enjoyed anywhere else, no matter how reputable.
You savor the memories, and taste the feelings, and, uh...
You're not even a local... How can anywhere here be dear to your heart?
Now put that travel guide away.
If you insist.
Do you have some kind of four-dimensional pocket? Where are you keeping all those weird books...?
And you keep taking us down alleyways too... Trying to lure out the people who attacked us earlier?
We're wards of the city's magistrate! What do we have to be afraid of?
Besides, they are hardly the upstanding sort! Rather than wait for them to come to us, I say we nip this in the bud before our rendezvous with the others.
The safest play would be to hole up in the Liang estate and contact the closest field office to send Operators to meet us in Shangshu... Hmm...
I've been meaning to ask, but are there many Yanese at Rhodes Island?
Now that you mention it, I don't think there's all that many, but it also feels like I run into them just about everywhere. It's strange—

lines.st: Rhodes Island? Rhodes Island...
Mm-hm.
Infected walking freely in the city?

lines.x: Benefactor, watch out—
Urgh—
Such a lightning-fast reaction.
What a surprise.
What is this about—
Mr. Nothing thinks fast and moves even faster, as do most kung fu practitioners.
However, the brawny man strikes more quickly than even the skip of Mr. Nothing's heartbeat, reaching his throat.
Don't try anything funny.
Nothing! Don't do it, mate!
Do you know your companion's identity? Did you know she belongs to an Infected organization?
Haha... Benefactor, to be quite honest, I'm afraid I couldn't do anything even if I wanted to...
What's more... This is a government official.
What a strange fan you wield. You are a master of your techniques, but you have yet to kill.
At least you are aware of your weaknesses.
Have we met...?
We have not.
However, it seems you two are somehow connected to the deputy minister of the Central Judicial Office.
You mean... Leizi?
Right! I saw you in our Operational Records before! You're...!
Taihe, Deputy Monitor Censor of the Department of Discipline and Supervision.
De-department of Discipline and Supervision...?!
Infected, what is your purpose here in Shangshu?
What happened at Kou-wu City? Why did Dusk leave the mountains?
And how is Rhodes Island involved?
(Benefactor, is this... about Miss Dusk?)
Keeping quiet, are we?
There is no need to probe Rhodes Island, sir. We've already gone through all the formalities... as Infected.
If you want to know about our Operators' private lives, though, there is truly nothing I can tell you.
Not that I know anything anyway.
Operators...?
A bestial Sui taking an office job? Unheard of.
It seems the Central Judicial Office was highly negligent in its handling of Rhodes Island.
So you tailed us, huh...?
Not at all.
But you won't let us go?
That depends on how cooperative you are.
No need to be so confrontational, Uncle Taihe.
Lord Zuo.
I've spoken with the other Candleholders to better understand the situation. These... Rhodes Island-related individuals are currently guests of the Shangshu magistrate.
They may be involved with the Mount Hui-ch'i incident, but they clearly mean no offense. We needn't come to blows.
My apologies. Uncle Taihe is usually a quiet man, but for whatever reason he is unusually agitated today.
I am Zuo Le, a... court Messenger. We will leave it at that.
I'm Kroos, a Rhodes Island Operator.
Chu Wuyou, otherwise known as Rhodes Island Operator Nothing. Although not yet officially.
Ms. Kroos, Mr. Nothing. Please accept my apologies.
I understand the transgression was Uncle Taihe's, but allow me to remind you:
If you are not too involved with those sisters, I suggest you put an end to that relationship while you still can.
Nian's my friend and coworker, and Dusk's my friend's pain-in-the-rear sister. So is that in the 'while we still can' stage or not?
To tell the truth... You've likely already crossed the line.
Thanks for the tip, then.
Heh.
Ms. Kroos, you're a woman of extraordinary courage and skill.
Now we have other business to attend to. Please excuse us.
I hope Magistrate Liang will give the Department of Discipline and Supervision an acceptable answer regarding the Rhodes Island affair.
Excuse us.
Benefactor! Are you okay?
Haha... That gave me such a start, I sprained my ankle. I'm okay, though.
Nothing, mate.
Hmm...
A Messenger accompanied by the Department of Discipline and Supervision's Deputy Monitir Censor as his retainer... What a load of poppycock. They really took us for fools.
It seems they're also well aware of the bizarre events in which we found ourselves at Kou-wu City and Mount Hui-ch'i.
Even a fool can glean that the sisters are no ordinary beings, but all this is a little... uh... beyond imagination.
Let's head back to the Liang estate first.
Now's not the time to be wandering around. We need to meet up with them as soon as possible...
Huh...?
What about him?
Nah... It was probably just some small critter.
*Sigh*.
'Tis not like you to sigh so much.
Are you so unhappy to see me tonight, my lord?
No.
Is something the matter?
Must I have urgent matters to seek your companionship?
No...
When shall we take to the mountains and see the plays?
The weather is still cold. I heard you've been ill as of late. You should be avoiding the mountains.
'Tis always the case in early spring. It makes me long for the hot tea served at Shuzhou Peak.
Right. I will go with you when I have the time.
That was your guest, was he not? You were never one to engage in small talk with Messengers.
Perhaps you might settle for 'leave the goods there. Stop wasting my time.'?
No, I doubt you would say that last part. After all, you are a man of proper etiquette, my lord.
Nothing escapes you, Miss Ning.
Where is he from?
Lungmen.
So far away! Ah... He came all this way to see you. I should not have intruded.
Not at all. He's not one to mind such trivialities.
My, how strange... To think you'd say that about someone.
Here I thought you would be the same aloof man to everyone.
*Cough*... I don't...
What is he doing here?
I... I entrusted some matters to him.
What sorts of matters?
All trivial.
So you invited an old friend from a thousand li away only for trivial matters...
If you will not tell me, I shan't ask.
However, this world is full of hardships. Such is life. If you were to anguish over each and every one of them...
You would only be overburdening yourself.
Hm?
Didn't Liang say this guest room was for me to use as I please?
Let's ignore the fact that you came into my room uninvited. Why are you making tea? MY tea, mind you.
It's not yours, it's Mr. Liang's.
And I made you a cup too. Satisfied?
So you plan to continue your accusations, Miss Du?
Take a guess.
I don't suppose you're bold enough to try anything in such a pretty mansion.
Why don't you start by telling me why you're here?
Heh, aren't you the confident one? Still carefree enough to make conversation with me.
Well, you made tea.
Alright, fine.
I'll get to the point. I'm here because I need your help.
Alright.
Huh?
Now that's odd. I thought you'd be more cautious. Why so agreeable?
Liang Xun let you into his home. What is there to worry about?
Do you know him?
I pleaded him to let me in, that's all... My father's always had a good relationship with the government.
I see.
Last time we met, I was going to steal your cup.
Not mine. Mr. Liang's.
Let him deal with the troublesome stuff. I only bother with whatever troubles come my way.
So you'll believe me...?
What reason do I have to hold a grudge against a young lady?
*Sigh*... All of us were young once, and all of us have had to put up an act just to save face.
—W-Who's saving face?! I was—
Did you get in a fight with the innkeeper?
Heh.
I'm starting to understand just what a terrifying person you are.
You seem lax and carefree, but you actually know everything like the back of your hand—People like you are the most terrifying.
At the very least, I wouldn't attack someone with a chair on our first meeting.
Now, tell me what this is about. And be honest, this is a negotiation.
Innkeeper Zheng... My father took on a job from a court noble. They want that wine cup of yours.
About what I figured, but earlier you were helping him with that. Why the sudden change of heart?
I won't go into the details. It's just... I don't want him to succeed.
So?
Will you not believe me if I don't tell you why?
Try not to ask questions that you already know the answer to. You want to come across as sincere.
If he succeeds, it doesn't matter how big a role I played. He'll make me take full credit for it.

The Escort Association has made itself a lot of enemies, and, for all the weight it has to throw around, it's stuck by the old rules even now that the Messenger industry has taken off. They're always left out for being slower to react than everyone else.
That's why my father always told me to keep my mouth shut, to hide my shortcomings.
He wants me to act like a frivolous playgirl who pisses away his riches. All so no one will give me a second thought.
Then, I'd secretly run errands for the court, both to curry favor and win over my lackeys.
Only then will he finally entrust me with the family business.
Well, that's an innkeeper for you. Always so calculating.
But I'm not part of the equation.
Whether it's the Du Yaoye who will inherit the Association, or the Miss Du who's always throwing money around, both are what others expect me to be.
So you want to take this chance to ensure your old man has to stay put and right the ship. That way you won't need to take over as the new innkeeper. Am I getting this right?
Not quite.
The royal court doesn't often come to the Association with a job. If this deal falls through, there is no way my father is going to hand me the keys to the Association just yet.
Most people my age look to me for a way out. I told them that if my father's deal falls through, the Association won't expect anything from us youngsters anymore.
But either way you're still waiting for your old man to pass the baton to you. What's the difference?
That's nothing more than an arrangement to keep us in line. Once I inherit the Association, I will have to follow the rules.
And the 'rules' go way beyond my father.
That's why you've come to me to... uh, help you put on this play of yours?
That it is.
Du Yaoye... Is that your name, my lady?
W-What about it?
'On this silent autumn night, lingering sorrows yet grapple my heart. A long night that never ceases.' Yaoye refers to the long night from that lovely poem.
Too bad, I prefer going out at twilight.
Since this is a deal though... what's in it for me?
There's somebody out there, from the royal court, who wants what you have. But you didn't let them have it. Do you understand what that means?
My friend never told me to hand it over. Besides, isn't he a member of said court?
Mr. Liang had you come all this way. He has something else for you, doesn't he?
Only trivial matters.
Whatever. I don't really care. It's just, now that it's come to this you won't be able to pretend you've got nothing to do with it anymore. No matter what you intend.
I can help you. At the very least, I can guarantee you'll make it back to Lungmen in one piece.
Fair point...
It's a deal, then.
It's a deal...
Heh, talking to you is easier than I expected.
Relax, I may not want to be the innkeeper, but all my friends my age have more than a few things to say about our elders. And all of them follow my orders.
You say you don't want to take over the Association, but aren't you doing the same thing...?
Times have changed.
Right, right.
I can't help but feel you're hiding something, but fine. It'll be nothing but trouble if they find me here.
Uhh...
Call me Lee.
—Alright, Lee. We'll meet again!
The innkeeper may seem a kind, amiable man, but his daughter...
Uncle Lee...
Hm?
You're visiting Uncle Liang, right?
Right... *Sigh*... I really don't want to go on this trip...
I'll keep an eye out for your dad.
With any luck, I might even drag him back here.
Hmm...
Thanks.
Oh, don't be like that... Don't expect too much either.
Yan is a big place, too big to find someone that easily.
Waai Fu...

Look, I need you to know. It doesn't matter if you're going to tell him off, hammer him with questions, or let bygones be bygones and fawn over him. If I do find him, you'll need to face him on your own.
It's only when you catch your fool of a dad's shadow that he'll turn back to look at you. That's the kind of man he is.
I'll be in Shangshu for a while. If I hear anything about him, I'll let you know.
And... once you're ready, come find me.
I understand.
*Sigh*.
That prick Liang Xun... neglecting his guest like this without so much as telling me his next move...
Hm... the tea isn't the best I've had, but the pot is quite the work of art. A fine teapot, indeed...
Hmm... A full calligraphy set too... Fairly old-fashioned for a guest room. I see Liang Xun's tastes haven't changed.
(Yawn)
Phew... That journey took it all out of me.
I'm past due for a break. After all that abuse I've taken from him, I'm pooped—
—Where am I?
Ao...
'The Shangshu Mountains are full of walkways, winding through its steep peaks among the clouds.'
A conspicuous sign stands here. The sign itself is new, and so are the letters written upon it. The words, however, are old.
The mountain is old, and so are the stone steps. The cable cars and the people, however, are all new.
I scratch my head and am surprised to find my hair less of a damp mess than expected. It is as though some indescribable sensation is enlightening my entire body and soul.
All one's vivid daytime memories quickly fade away. Yet, they thoughtfully drift back when needed.
I decide to make my way up.
I am not sure why I do so, but even if these steps took me immediately heavenward, I would still ascend.
As if in response to my sincerity, I find yet another sign around the corner.
A new sign, this time accompanied by new words. It resembles one that advertises scenic spots for tourists.
'Zuanjiang Peak'
Zuanjiang Peak... Is that where I am?
Hello—Anybody home—
Hello... Anybody home...
Fine. Now that I'm here, might as well make the best of this.
(There seems to be a pavilion in the distance.)
(Strange... I feels like I've seen this place before...)
We don't often get visitors here. Are you heading to the pavilion?
Oh, no... I'm just going to take a peek.
What is this place...?
Zuanjiang Peak.
One of Shangshu's Three Mounts and Seventeen Peaks?
Eighteen, not Seventeen.
The path to the pavilion isn't an easy one. Someone must have had a lot of free time to build it up there.
Looks like there's somebody up there though...?
Of course. Why else would you build a pavilion?
And you're sweeping snow here?
Correct.
There's nobody here.
They'll only come after the snow is cleared.
Who will?
Who knows, but you're here now, aren't you?
Isn't the one at the pavilion beckoning you?
Watch your step.
The path is slippery when it's snowy.
Is everyone here?
Correct.
The masters won't let us in on the fun, and the innkeeper is always busy with his customers. The only ones missing are those still sitting around waiting for table scraps.
Are you sure we don't have to listen to I-Innkeeper Zheng...? Can we back out?
Huh? You still wanna follow him? Might as well quit martial arts and work as one of his cooks!

All the logistics companies have gotta be swimming in dough, with how busy they are. But, no, the old man is so in love with his rules that he'd rather open a restaurant than compete! He must be senile or something!
To Innkeeper Zheng, a restaurant is an upstanding business, while attaching the word 'logistics' to the Xingyu name would merely sully the Association. He'd never accept that.
And why does he think that?
How would I know?

We all know old man Zheng's scared of everything these days. After all those years running the Escort Association, only now when he's about to kick the bucket does he realize how many people he's offended for nothing.
At this rate, the Association is done for, with or without anyone kicking down our doors.
But this is a huge job from the court, right? You sure it's okay to screw it up on purpose?
This will only work because it's a huge job. I know what I'm doing.
Help! Thief!
Don't let him get away!
Uh... is that part of your plan too, Miss Du?
Of course not! Let's go!
It seems your friend is asleep.
Well, it's getting awfully late...
After nothing but small talk?
Then what of the play...?
We'll discuss this another time. You should rest. Try not to go outside so often.
You need to take care of yourself.
I visit because there are matters more pressing to me than my health.
You mean the plays...?
Mm-hm.
I can't leave while I still have duties to attend to.
Is that your way of berating me for idling about all day?
Of course not.
Help! Thief!
Hm... A thief?
How bizarre... A thief at the Liang estate?
You head on inside. I will take a look.
What is going on here?
Oh, Mr. Liang. I just arrived myself.
You two are Lee's guests, and that makes you my guests as well. As your host, I apologize for the disturbance.
M-Mr. Liang. I was just preparing to leave for the day, when all of a sudden I saw a silhouette up on the wall...
A silhouette?
Y-Yes, and it looked like it was holding a box...
To Lee's room! Hurry!
The lady sweeping snow was right, it was a difficult climb. And now that I made it, the person I saw is gone.
Hey, are you still here—?

lines.st: I am.

lines.x: What is this place anyway...?

lines.st: The pinnacle of your hike. In other words, the summit.

lines.x: Why did you summon me here?

lines.st: You are the one who called me.

lines.x: I did?
Right...
I'm here... to reminisce with you, about the old days.

lines.st: How about a match?

lines.x: I'll pass... There's no suspense to it, much less any enjoyment.

lines.st: Oh, looking down on me?
So you only want a drinking buddy, then?

lines.x: I'm here to give you a small gift.

lines.st: It's been decades since we last met. Forget the gift; it's the thought that counts.

lines.x: Only a few decades. Like the snap of a finger.

lines.st: What's this...?

lines.x: A pair, one black and one white, like light and shadow coexisting in harmony.
Pick one, and I'll keep the other.

lines.st: Hm.

lines.x: What's the matter? Not to your liking?

lines.st: You can fool others, and you can fool our other siblings, but you can't fool me.
You...
Are you not afraid of losing this whole game?

lines.x: I am, yet also not.
Besides, I didn't exactly have a choice—

lines.st: —Lee!
Lee—!

lines.x: Hm... Was that... all a dream?
Are you okay?
I'm not so old that you need to worry about me kicking the bucket in my sleep... What's the matter?
Sir, someone just spotted a thief making off with a case that looked just like yours!
Is the case gone?
You mean the wine cup...? Don't worry. It's right here.
How did you...?
The box only contains an ordinary teapot I took from the cupboard, painted black with ink.
I'm not stupid enough to leave it out in the open, knowing someone is after it.
My good man, you are every bit the cunning genius that Kroos described.
Mr. Liang.
Someone out there is bold enough to provoke the magistrate for an old antique. If every thief were this brazen, the L.G.D. would only need to guard street food stands.
So isn't it time you told me what this is about?
I will tell you everything you need to know.
Which also coincides with the other matter I need your help with.
Find its owner.
I see... Some actual detective work this time.
Before that though... Mr. Nothing, where's that benefactor of yours, Kroos?
Uh... D-Did she chase after them?!
I'll go check on her!
(I followed them all the way to this night market, but isn't it a bit too lively?)
(Did I lose 'em?)
(Let me check this direction...)
Oops, my bad—
—You're from this afternoon—
I don't suppose you'd believe me if I said I had nothing to do with this?
I haven't even said anything yet.
But put yourself in my shoes. Soon as I arrive in Shangshu, I run into someone trying to rob a friend. And now again. Would YOU believe that's a coincidence?
Don't worry though, I'll give you plenty of time to work out your excuse.
Hmph.
Might as well save my breath, then!
I'll go this way. You check over there.
Right then! I'm counting on you, Brother Lee.
Hold on.
Is something the matter?
What's that... beside your feet?
Hm? I don't see anything.
Must be my eyes playing tricks... Maybe someone's pet?
Let's go.
This isn't it...
If things were really so simple, then I would find myself worried about Mr. Liang's eye for talent.
Mr. Zheng, do we know anything about the visitor from Lungmen yet?
He's an old friend of Liang Xun's.
Anything special about him?
We have a few unclear leads, but I can at least guarantee that this affair has nothing to do with Lord Wei.
That's my father's greatest worry taken care of.
True though, considering the sort of methods that Lord Wei of Lungmen usually employs... If he were involved, we wouldn't be sitting here enjoying tea right now.
I've heard many anecdotes about him over the years. It's a shame that I've never had the chance to visit Lungmen to see his exploits with my own eyes.
I'm surprised that you'd show such a keen interest in someone like him.
I walked the world of kung fu in my younger years, and I've heard he's fine swordsman. That's all.
I see. It's true that he's a swordsman... As for his swordsmanship, though, all I know is rumors.
In any case, can we assume it was Mr. Liang himself who chose to involve the Lungmenite?
Undoubtedly.
Hm... He is the only remaining variable, and also Mr. Liang's only trump card.
Mr. Liang should've been on our side. The man he chose should not have tried to deceive me either. It was an illogical move.
Mr. Liang doesn't have much of a choice himself. If only... *Sigh*... No, it wouldn't have mattered.
Isn't her presence in Shangshu the reason everything has gotten so out of hand?
The Three Mounts and Seventeen Peaks... A place graced by the presence of a deity.

In eras past, Shangshu was a place where many dreamed. Not only people but all inanimate things. As the saying goes, all things in the world can be deified with souls, and all words in the realm can be summarized in a sigh.

According to legend, a millennium ago the Yanese towns were scattered all over the country. It was only when our Shangshu forebears all collectively saw the same dream that they chose to settle down on these lands.
The legends of Shangshu have always been so magnificent.
Outsiders may see it as mere legend, but to the locals it is history.
The Operator from the medical company... Kroos, was it?
If it wasn't for your help, she would likely have caught up to me. You have my thanks, Mr. Zheng.
You once saved my daughter from having her hotheadedness lead to disaster. It is only natural for me to come to your aid.
At the end of the day, Rhodes Island is an unknown. What do you think, Innkeeper?
They are not a part of the game.
They are nonetheless standing very close to the table.
Hmm... I rarely see young a woman with such fine techniques, though. Seeing her chase after you gave me a good idea of her abilities.
And what's your assessment?
She may be young, but she's a battle-hardened veteran.
Hoh? Did you arrive at that conclusion as Zheng the Innkeeper or Zheng Qingyue, the Asker to the Frost?
Both.
You haven't once looked away from that fake cup.
I'm only reminiscing... reminiscing about the goblet's original form.
When we crossed that wilderness all those years ago... we ran into a group of bandits. None of them spoke Yanese, and we hadn't the slightest idea where they came from.

They blew up the caravan and tried to steal our wares, though our Escort Association colleagues stopped them. Still, there were far too many, all obsessed with our wares, and they followed us for two hundred li.
It was raining. Visibility was low, and there was nary a light in sight. The bandits, who knew the terrain much better, followed us like shadows.
We were afraid to counterattack, lest they have some kind of underhanded trick up their sleeves. We were too afraid to run either, lest we couldn't shake them.
From what I heard, the Sui Regulator once secretly commissioned the renowned Xingyu Escort Association to transport an item, only for it to be lost in an accident en route.
That was my fault.
I'm afraid I'm not very good with comforting words, Mr. Zheng.
I simply never expected that the next time I met a crossbowman of such horrifying prowess would be in my hometown, in the dead center of the city, under a well-lit night, all for the same goblet.
I truly... cannot begin to explain how I feel.
How did you escape then, Mr Zheng?
When do you mean?
Oh... The incident you were just talking about, of course. My father always tells me that I am still young and inexperienced, and should learn from my elders.
If you would continue to share your story with me, I'll gladly listen.
Luck was on my side that night...
Two of my men were hurt. Good men. Neither of them made it.
The Association's rule is to 'save the goods, then the men.' We all knew the risks when we joined the business.
But with the injured dead and the goods threatened, that actually freed us to do as we pleased.
It was pouring that night. Helped to wash the blood off our blades.
So you just need me to find the cup's owner?
Correct.
By owner, you mean a buyer or collector?
Not quite. It would be a much simpler matter if that were the case.
Hm, that's strange. This cup was shockingly cheap, but according to the black market appraisal I stole from the smugglers, it seems...
It's an actual antique, through and through.
Who's the man of the hour then?
I don't know their name... Nor anything else, really. Their gender, their race, their age...
Well... Where am I supposed to start then...? Are you even sure they're in Shangshu?
That much is certain, and only that.
How do you know? Suppose our friend decides to take a trip, wouldn't that make this a huge waste of time?
No, they are in Shangshu. I can guarantee you that.
If they had left Shangshu... I would have been notified.
Strange. So you can monitor them, but you can't locate them?
That's right...
Are they some kind of monster or enlightened deity?
You'll know what I mean once you see them.
So they must leave some kind of indication that they're here... Is this the kind of funny business government officials have to deal with these days?
Fine, I won't probe any further if you don't want to tell me. It's just, that's not much of a lead in a town this big...
They're most likely hiding in one of the Three Mounts.
How do you figure?
Our friend is unpredictable, but every once in a while they show up at the mountainside liquor shops to buy alcohol, with no regularity whatsoever.
But they've never come down to the valleys, nor have they ever appeared in the nomadic cities. In which case, they're most likely somewhere in the mountains.
Very well, then. The Three Mounts and Seventeen Peaks it is. Sounds like a fun trip.
Legend has it that our friend is an appreciator of fine weather and scenery. And they enjoy lounging in pavilions and writing poetry all the more.
Legend, huh?
It is more than mere legend, actually...
So you're familiar with them?
Not exactly.
In the end, you may just have to try your luck, troublesome as it sounds.
Do we have a deadline?

I'd appreciate it if you'd take care of this as soon as possible. As you can tell, a few too many people are already mixed up in this affair. The longer it continues, the greater the chance it will get even more out of hand.
As for the cup... Seeing as someone has their eyes on it, we should keep it in the safety of my estate.
Amazing, Mr. Liang. First you had me bring it to you across thousands of li, then you want me to find its owner, and now you won't even let me hold it?
You're really putting me in quite a bind. Perhaps next time you'll arrange for me to help you from the comfort of Lungmen?
This is a sign of my trust in you.
It's true though that if someone has their eyes on it, then keeping it on my person is asking for trouble.
As for the Xingyu Inn...
We're investigating it. Be careful on your way. If you need help, I can arrange for someone to accompany you.
No need. A bodyguard tagging along would just make me stand out. It's trouble, and you know it.
Are you certain you'll be fine on your own, though...?
Sure, as long as you aren't hiding anything else from me.
I'm looking for someone, not investigating a scary smuggler ring in Lungmen. How much trouble can it be?
At least talk to Master Shen. As a veteran boatman, he can help you get anywhere in Shangshu.
Very well.
Incidentally...
Is there a Zuanjiang Peak among Shangshu's Three Mounts and Seventeen Peaks?
Urgh! Just how stubborn are you, bitch?!
Well, you're the only lead I've got. Not letting you go.
You're all alone, with no lackeys. And are you sure you want to start a fight in a bustling part of town like this?
Hmph...
How much did that thief from Lungmen pay you to cover him like this?
I don't know how things got to this point, but Mr. Lee is one of our most important partners.
Partners? Business then. And you believe him, just like that? You sure he's not swindling you?
I trust Rhodes Island, so I trust him. Simple as that.
Oh...? Seems we won't be coming to an agreement, then.
Looks like.
But Innkeeper Zheng also said not to get you any more mixed up in this than you already are.
Oh... Judging by your reaction, Mr. Lee got it right, then? You're in this with the innkeeper?
You... Playing me for a fool, are you?! You're just some shady girl from who-knows-where! How dare you make a fool of me!
Fine, I'll make sure you pay for this!
So we're doing this for real, huh?
Alright, I've actually got a few things to ask you—Nothing to do with Mr. Lee, but still.
Regarding Miss Du...
She may be my daughter, but I'm ashamed to say she is still very immature.
No, she's your family. I am in no position to meddle in your household affairs.
How many inns and restaurants have you opened here in your town?
Hohoh... I've spent my entire my life savings on this business. Fortunately, my old pals take care of me these days. The connections I made over the years finally came in handy.

*Sigh*... While it takes connections to survive in this business, of course, in the end it's all down to how capable you are. Being a businessman is another thing entirely. It takes skills to start a business, but connections to build it up.
No doubt you are already well aware of that though.
Not at all. My father always tells me what youngsters like me lack isn't persistence or conviction. It's an understanding of everything that surrounds us.
Wise words. Indeed, being holed up in a castle in the clouds is no way to live.
Still, General Zuo is a good father, but it doesn't matter what your father thinks. What is truly important is... what you think.
Don't worry about that too much. When you are young, there is no shame in letting your frustration, curiosity, or anger get the better of you.
On the contrary, young man, you don't want to lose your edge too early.
Thank you for your advice.
*Sigh*... My advice wasn't just for you. It may sound simple, but it is no simple matter. I had to hear it for myself, all the same.
My daughter... What a headache she is.
––Hraaaagh!
Hm. Nice moves.
Spare me the act... It didn't even connect.
How come you're like a whole different person when you're fighting...? My mate Mr. Nothing is like that too. Are all you Yanese like that?
I don't want to hurt anyone, that's all.
You called us thieves, didn't you? Aren't you going a little too easy on a thief?
Save it.
That's not going to fly this time.
Heh... How about we stop beating around the bush, then?
Sure thing, I'm all for it. Gotta warn you, though. I'm not exactly speaking for Mr. Lee here.
Doesn't matter. Neither you nor that conman are the honest sort. The sooner we get this conversation over with, the better.
Who did you say you were again?
Codename Kroos, an Operator currently on deployment from Rhodes Island.
So Mr. Nothing is that guy's codename?
Something like that. He's not quite on the job just yet, though.
You work behind fake names, hiding knives beneath your smiles. What a sketchy lot.
Whoa... Never heard that one before...
Someone hired us to take the cup back from the Lungmenite.
Yanese is a tricky language, and I can't say I've had time to master it, but wouldn't you say using the phrase 'take back' here is a little too convenient?
I trust Mr. Lee. If you really had a grievance, or if this were all a misunderstanding, did it really have to come to blows?
This is the Yanese heartland, not Siracusa or Bolívar... violence won't get you anywhere.
I have my reasons to open with hostilities instead of courtesies... That said, do you seriously not know anything?
Oh, so now I'm the one who's getting played for a fool, huh?
If you really don't know, you'd best stay out of this and dig no further. I've already spoken with the Lungmenite. If you insist on sticking your nose into this, you'll ruin it for everyone.
Well, aren't a righteous one?
What we are doing is righteous. Naturally, our reasons are righteous too.
Now that I've told you this much, I might as make this clear, whether you believe me or not:
—I didn't rob the Liang estate.
But you know who did.
Perhaps.
You say you've already talked to Mr. Lee... so how about a truce? Can you give us an explanation we'd accept?
An explanation...?
You said the Lungmenite was hired by Magistrate Liang to deliver a goblet from Lungmen—
—So the one who hired the Escort Association could be someone even Liang Xun is afraid to offend.
What?
It's beyond complicated. Don't count on me to explain it to you.
Miss Du!
Benefactor! Are you alright?!
You punk! You here to get in our way too?!
I haven't even said anything yet!
Get him, boys!
Hmph... That's enough chit-chat for one night. Excuse me.
Get out of here. Stop badgering that guy already!
—Alright!
Hey, first you say you'll come get me, and now you don't?! Must you always give me such trouble?
—Hold on!
You ain't half bad, and you know how to listen. So I'll leave you with a piece of advice—
—The mountain rapids are turbulent and steep! Stay out of this!
Benefactor.
I'm fine... Don't worry.
Miss Du, are you okay?
Aren't those two working with the Lungmenite? Why don't we just tell them the truth?
I know what I'm doing.
Oh...
Old Man Zheng wants to dump this on us... Well, that's not going to happen.
I've got a deal with the Lungmenite. The old banner has flown long enough. It's time to switch it out for a new one.
Midday. The skies are clear, as though the heavy rains the night before had been nothing but a lie.
The second tallest of Shangshu's Three Mounts is Mount Juqi. Juqi is home to only two of the Seventeen Peaks, the lesser of which is Nini Peak.
There are twenty two hundred and twenty two steps leading up to Nini Peak. While this may seem like a fascinating coincidence, additional steps were added specifically for the gimmick.
The steps lead heavenward through the clouds. The higher you get, the fewer visitors can be found. A lush, green mountain, rarely ever trodden by footsteps.
A few men can be seen here and there; all of them appear to be pole-carriers on first glance.
This is no Mount T'ien, but truly, what a fine summit.
How do you suppose they built a teahouse up here in the mountains, when the roads aren't so much as paved?
Hmm. By hand. How else?
On our way up here, we saw a pole-carrier walking down the mountain, didn't we? They do this every day, carrying anything from foodstuffs to bricks up from the base of the mountain.
From what I hear, a single trip takes them a good few hours. It's very toilsome work. Even today, some remote mountains still rely on pole-carriers for their survival...
Can't they build a ropeway for some cable cars or something?
First they'd need a plot of land to build it on, wouldn't they?
Right, not that I know the first thing about building ropeways... Those guys really are amazing, though.
They probably rake in quite a bit dough, don't they?
Not until recently. I saw on the news not too long ago that the Shangshu magistrate thinks very highly of the coolies. The government's even footing the bill for the teahouses' tea... Uhh?
Pardon... 'Scuse me.
Noon tea.
You got it, Master Shang.
Haven't seen you for a couple days. How've you been?
Same old.
What were you up to?
Saw my son.
Oh... It's that time again... I'd forgotten.
If you'd told me sooner, we could've gone together.
Don't worry about it...
He likes it quiet.
*Sigh*... It's been years now... I'm only saying this because we know each other so well, and I mean no disrespect, but well...
We can't be stuck in the past.
You speaking for Zheng Qingyue?
Come on, you know he personally invested in all the teahouses here.
You're on his payroll. You speak for him. No offense taken.
That's half my own opinion too.
Half-offended then.
It's been years already. Just hear me out.
Have some tea and think it through.
Isn't that the man we saw go down the mountain earlier...?
I-I think so? It hasn't been that long, has it? He's back already?
He and his buddies must have some kind of relay system so he only has to go down halfway. That's the only plausible explanation.
No good... Can't get through to 'em.
There's no response, which means Nian and Dusk aren't in Shangshu yet... They might not be following the mission directives.
Benefactor...
Yeah, I know what you're about to say.
I'm hardly a clever man, and I certainly can't claim to remember everything I see, but if my eyes did not deceive me...
The case that Mr. Lee handed to Mr. Liang... carried the same pattern as the trinket that Nian had.
Must have something to do with them suddenly breaking off from the team.
What should we do?
Hm... Well, I kind of saw this coming too.
I talked to Lava, you see. Asked her to contact the Operators near Shangshu and have them come get us as soon as she made it to the office.
Oh? So you came prepared?
Nian was acting awfully dodgy the whole way. I figured she was up to something.
Are you suggesting we stay here for the time being then?
We bumped into Mr. Lee. Naturally we have to help him. It's in the contract and everything.
I see. You'll handle this fairly and impartially, while remaining vigilant and conscientious. You continue to command my utmost respect, benefactor.
But Mr. Lee's a smart man... He never bothered requesting Rhodes Island's assistance ever since we ran into him at the inn. Maybe he's been trying to tell us not to get involved.
If this really does have to do with Nian, though... Not to mention Taihe and Zuo Le...
Anyway, we'll check out the rendezvous point when the time comes. Nian's probably gonna be a no-show, but Dusk isn't that kind of girl. We might see her there.
Right. Dusk is likely to drop a hint or two, at least.
*Sigh*.
No matter how I slice it, though, sometimes things are just too—
—Too coincidental?
Nothing, mate, you didn't have any particular reason for choosing that inn, right?
Oh, you jest. Suppose I had an ulterior motive, how could I have counted on a Gloompincer knocking my car over and then us running into each other?
Then just who is it that made everything line up so perfectly?
Oh...
Benefactor... that WAS a jest, right? A joke, a jape? You couldn't possibly be suspecting me, could you? Benefactor?!
Dad.
What's the matter...?
I thought I was in charge of this whole thing?
Someone has to clean up the mess.
If you can do it, then clearly I could too—
Wrong.
Even if you could, you can't. As long as you're still Du Yaoye and not Du, Head of the Escort Association, there is absolutely no way.
Why?!
Do you still fail to realize why I didn't seize the target when he was at the inn? Why I stopped you, even?
It was because of that Cautus with the skills, and that Nothing dude—
It seems you still have much to learn.
What do you mean...?
You have no eye for people.
Dad!
Do you want me to inherit the Association, or do you want me to be the naive Miss Du my whole life?!
An escort has many enemies. You don't understand.
And so I should continue to be the stupid, ignorant daughter of the Association's head?
I'm supposed to let everyone think that Zheng, Head of the Escort Association, is now just an honest businessman, and I'm a successor who's not up to snuff?
You know that's not who I am.
Ye, my girl...
Do you know the size of the bone the young people at the Escort Association have to pick with you?
What do they know?
And what do you know?
You're trying to wash your hands of the business, yet you don't even realize how long the younger generation has been unhappy with the current state of the Association.
Then are you telling me, in all honesty, that starting a new association with those inexperienced disciples is what you truly want?
Is that who you are now?
All I know is that you made me do this, Dad. And that if I succeed, not only will they all bow down to me, I'll have a fine new addition to my rap sheet.
I'm worried that you might not have what it takes.
And I'm even more worried that you're actually trying to mess this up.
I don't know what you're talking about...
If you've changed your mind, then hurry up and tell me. And if you haven't, then...
Stop worrying about me.
How did it go, Miss...? What did the innkeeper say?
Hmph, how do you think it went?
Zheng got played. They switched it out with a decoy.
At least we don't have to screw this one up ourselves. Sounds like he's already on top of it.
Not necessarily.
He knows what we're up to.
H-Huh?
He knows? What should we do, then? If our masters ground us, we can't do nothing!
Solving a problem like this can take a while, but creating a new one is simple enough.
I had this same thought when I first visited Shangshu years ago, but the people here are really quite something.

From Xinluan to Liuyun, how many wondrous peaks and precipitous paths will we have to cross? The city is dispersed along the mountain chain, and there are settlements scattered all across. What a wondrous sight.
